coredata.dc:title: ZnO/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites derived from Moringa oleifera gum: An improved catalytic vitiation of methylene blue dye

Latif, Nida

Kanwal, Aqsa
Hussain, Shahid
Habila, Mohamed A.
Iqbal, Amjad
Manavalan, Rajesh Kumar
Farooq, Nosheen
coredata.dc:description: The present study reports on the enhanced photocatalytic and chemocatalytic performance of ZnO/carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposites for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. Carbon quantum dots were prepared by employing Moringa oleifera gum powder as a carbon source through the hydrothermal process. ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites were synthesized by dispersing ZnO nanoparticles into carbon quantum dot solution. The obtained samples were characterized by using techniques like FTIR, UV, and SEM with EDX analysis. When the methylene blue (MB) dye was exposed to visible light at room temperature, the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites photocatalyst exhibited more photocatalytic and chemocatalytic activity than the pure ZnO nanoparticle photocatalyst. Antibacterial activity and cytotoxic assay of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites were also investigated. In addition to protecting the environment, the purpose of this research is to create a new visible-light photocatalyst for the effective treatment of organic wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis
ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites
Degradation

Characterization of CQDs, ZnO and ZnO/CQDs
XRD diffraction study of CQDs and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue dye
Chemo catalytic degradation of methylene blue

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2024R441), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. One of the authors, Rajesh Kumar Manavalan thanks the contract no 40/is2 and gratefully acknowledges research funding from the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation (Ural Federal University project within the Priority 2030 Program).

This work was funded by the Researchers Supporting Project Number ( RSP2024R441 ), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia . One of the authors, Rajesh Kumar Manavalan thanks the contract no. 40/is2 and gratefully acknowledges research funding from the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation (Ural Federal University project within the Priority 2030 Program) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of CQDs.
UV spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) ZnO, (c) ZnO/CQDs.
Calculated band gap from UV–Visible absorption spectrum.
FTIR spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) ZnO, (c) ZnO/CQDs.
XRD Patterns of CQDs and ZnO/CQDs Nanocomposite.
UV-visible spectrum showing the photocatalytic degradation of MB in the presence of (a) ZnO/CQDs, visible light (b) ZnO, visible light and (C) alone visible light irradiation.
Mechanism of photocatalytic activity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites.
UV-visible spectrum showing the degradation pattern of Methylene Blue Dye in the presence of (a) ZnO/CQDs, NaBH4 (b) ZnO, NaBH4 and (c) NaBH4 alone.
Mechanism of chemo-catalytic activity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites.
Stability of ZnO/CQDs composites for (a) chemocatalytic and (b) photocatalytic Degradation.
EDX apparent concentration and weight% of CQDs.
Comparison of Antibacterial activity of ZnO/CQDs with positive control.
Investigation of Hemolytic activity of ZnO/CQDs.

head.ce:title: ZnO/carbon quantum dots nanocomposites derived from Moringa oleifera gum: An improved catalytic vitiation of methylene blue dye

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study reports on the enhanced photocatalytic and chemocatalytic performance of ZnO/carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposites for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye. Carbon quantum dots were prepared by employing Moringa oleifera gum powder as a carbon source through the hydrothermal process. ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites were synthesized by dispersing ZnO nanoparticles into carbon quantum dot solution. The obtained samples were characterized by using techniques like FTIR, UV, and SEM with EDX analysis. When the methylene blue (MB) dye was exposed to visible light at room temperature, the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites photocatalyst exhibited more photocatalytic and chemocatalytic activity than the pure ZnO nanoparticle photocatalyst. Antibacterial activity and cytotoxic assay of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites were also investigated. In addition to protecting the environment, the purpose of this research is to create a new visible-light photocatalyst for the effective treatment of organic wastewater.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, environment pollution has become a serious issue for human health due to fast industrial expansion [1,2]. In order to solve this problem, many effective techniques were used to remove pollutants. Of all the methods that are available In the areas of wastewater treatment, air purification, and other environmental management, photocatalysis is the most effective, durable, and economical technique [3,4]. Carbon nanomaterials have gained a lot of interest in the field of photocatalysis because of their great electron transfer capacity, good stability, low cost and cytotoxicity [5] but their combination with semiconductor metal oxide such as TiO2, ZnO, SnO2, ZrO2, Bi2O3, and Fe2O3, provides improved photocatalytic activity, charge separation and charge recombination reduction [4].

An important phase in the effective decomposition of water contaminants is the creation of commercially available and light absorbing semiconductor photocatalyst [6,7]. The band gap of a semiconductor is an important characteristic in determining its suitability for a particular photocatalytic reaction [8]. Mostly TiO2 has been used as a photocatalyst for degradation of dyes and pollutants in wastewater streams due to outstanding properties such as wide band gap (3.2 eV), high activity and long-term stability [9,10]. ZnO is considered an effective photocatalyst because ZnO has large band gap, low cost and highly biocompatible. Numerous techniques have been employed to enhance photocatalytic performance of ZnO, including as coupling with secondary semiconductors, doping with transition metals or nonmetal elements, and modification with metal and carbon nanoparticles. After considering the aforementioned points, it seems that using ZnO in conjunction with CQDs is a viable method of increasing photocatalytic activity using visible light as source [11].

A recent development in luminous carbon materials with a high oxygen content is carbon quantum dots [12]. While purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes, Xu et al. discovered carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in 2004 [13]. The remarkable properties of carbon quantum dots, such as their exceptional photo stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and ease of modification, excellent water dispersibility and high sensitivity have attracted a lot of interest. CQDs have been applied in drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensing and detecting analytes [14,15]. Moreover, carbon quantum dots modified with metal oxide or CQDs-based composites show superior catalytic activity as compared to single nanoparticles because electron transport at the surface of carbon quantum dots is more efficient [16].

Typically, CQDs consist of heteroatomic functional groups with heterogeneous distribution of sp2 and sp3 carbon domains [17]. CQDs center is the pure carbon core containing predominantly of graphene-like sp2-hybridization and consist of diamond-type sp3 hybridization of amorphous carbon atoms [18,19]. Three types of carbon quantum dots are distinguished: polymer dots (PDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs) [20]. GQDs are single-sheet π-conjugated carbon nanodots and tiny graphene fragments with a higher horizontal dimension than height. Carbon nanodots are highly carbonized nanoparticles of a tiny size having specific chemical groups on their surfaces. Nanodots are amorphous and quasi-spherical that do not show quantum confinement and divided into carbon nanoparticles. Polymers with cross-links made from combined linear or monomers are referred to as PDs.

There has been a lot of attention lately in the synthesis of carbon quantum dots from green precursors [21] due to their well-defined optical characteristics, high level of biocompatibility, facile synthesis, less toxicity as well as variable precursor sources [22]. There are two types of synthetic C-dot methods; top-down and bottom-up approaches [23]. Top-down synthesis is the process in which bulk carbon precursors are broken into carbon nanoparticles. This method includes electrochemical oxidation, arc discharge, laser ablation and plasma treatment [22,23]. Bottom-up synthesis refers to the production of CQDs from molecular precursors such as glucose, citric acid, xylem, or resin by using microwave assisted strategy, hydrothermal treatment, thermal and template method [24,25] Among these methods, hydrothermal treatment is very simple and cost effective method that uses different carbon sources, including carbohydrates, organic compounds, and even waste biomass [26,27].

The intrinsic properties of semiconductors that might be used for a variety of applications could be adjusted by the fabrication of composites using metal oxide semiconductors. For example, using a simple chemical method, Xin et al. synthesized CQDs/ZnO nanoflower composites and examined their photocatalytic activity by degrading rhodamine B when exposed to visible light. This outcome showed that the nanocomposites have a maximum efficiency of 98 %, which is more than pure ZnO [28]. Bozetine et al. reported ZnO/carbon quantum dots (ZnO/CQDs) nanocomposites synthesized hydrothermally. The photocatalytic activity of these nanocomposites was investigated by degrading Rhodamine B (RhB) in a sunlight-like environment. As compared to pure ZnO, the degradation efficiency was higher, as 94 % degradation [29]. Velumani et al. prepared ZnO/CDs nanocomposite by hydrothermal method. Methylene blue (MB) was used to measure the photocatalytic degradation activity of pure ZnO and ZnO/CDs nanocomposites under UV–visible light irradiation. Maximum photocatalytic MB dye degradation efficiency was demonstrated by ZnO/CDs nanocomposites as 96 %, significantly higher than that of pure ZnO, 63 % [3].

Herein, we present the production of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites, which demonstrate improved photocatalytic and chemocatalytic efficacy for the vitiation of methylene blue dye under visible light and exhibits antibacterial activity and cytotoxic assay. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a simple, green one step hydrothermal carbonization method from Moringa oleifera gum powder as carbon source, then the synthesis of ZnO/CQDs was carried out by sonication method.

Moringa gum was got from the local market. Ethanol, zinc nitrate, potassium hydroxide, methylene blue, sodium borohydride. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Company. During the entire experimental work, deionized water was utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal carbonization process was used for the preparation of Carbon Quantum Dots. A suitable amount of (3 g) powdered Moringa Oleifera gum was added into 60 ml deionized water and stirred for 24 h. Following the formation of the gum solution, it was transferred to a Teflon line stainless steel autoclave and heated to 190 °C for 16 hr. Upon reaction completion the carbon quantum dots were allowed to cool naturally at room temperature. Whatmann filter paper with a pore size of 0.22 μm was then used to filter the brownish solution to make sure accurate filtering. To remove brown particles at room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged for 30 min at 12,000 rpm. The supernatant containing carbon quantum dots was evaporated by rotary evaporator and dried in vacuum dessicator for 24 hr. Ultimately, the product was dried and kept in storage at 0 °C for additional analysis and use. The schematic illustration for the CQD preparation is displayed in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnO nanoparticles by precipitation method
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO nanoparticles were produced by the direct precipitation method using potassium hydroxide and zinc nitrate as precursors. In this investigation, aqueous solutions of zinc nitrate (Zn (NO3)2·6H2O) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) were prepared using deionized water (0.2 M and 0.6 M, respectively). The KOH solution was gently added to the zinc nitrate solution with vigorous stirring and 80 °C temperature for 4 hrs. After completing the reaction white suspension was formed. Next, ethanol and deionized water were used to wash this white suspension after it had been centrifuged for 20 min at 10,000 rpm. The obtained product was dried at 60 °C in air atmosphere.
ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites were successfully synthesized by dispersing 0.05 g ZnO nanoparticles into 5 ml carbon quantum dot solution. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min and dried at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, ZnO and ZnO/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Characterization of the possible functional group of dried solid powder CQDs, ZnO, and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites was performed with Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Optical properties of aqueous BCDs were collected by UV–VIS absorption spectrum (Agilent Cary 60), and fluorescence spectrum were recorded using a Cary Eclipse Spectrophotometer. Morphology, size, and elemental analysis of CQDs was investigated with scanning electron microscope coupled with energy dispersive X-Ray (SEM-EDX) (Nova NanoSEM 450).

By degrading methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation (tungsten lamp), the photocatalytic activity of produced ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and ZnO nanoparticles (samples) was evaluated. In order to perform the photocatalysis treatment, 60 mL of MB dye solution were added, starting at a concentration of 25 ppm. Then, 75 mg of each photocatalyst-pure ZnO nanoparticle and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite were dispersed into the MB solution. The adsorption-desorption equilibrium between MB dye and photocatalysts was first achieved by stirring the mixture in the dark for 30 min. After that, for 150 min at room temperature, this solution was exposed to radiation from a tungsten lamp. During the irradiation, every 30 min, 4 mL solution is taken and centrifuged (12,000 rpm) to remove the catalysts. The filtrate was analyzed by recording the highest differences in the absorption bands (663 nm) [30,31] The percentage degradation of reaction mixture was calculated by using following equation.
Degradation
( % )
=
(
1 −
C t
C 0
)
× 100
where, C0 is the initial MB absorbance at 663 nm, Ct is the absorbance after various intervals of irradiation time.

ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and ZnO nanoparticles (samples) were tested for their chemocatalytic activity by employing NaBH4 as a reducing agent to degrade methylene blue (MB). Herein, To the freshly synthesized 150 μL of 100 mM NaBH4, 2.5 ml of 25 μM methylene blue was added. Then 200 μL of samples (10 mg/ml) were added into the above solution. The UV-visible spectrophotometer was used to record the time-dependent catalytic degradation at a wavelength of 663 nm.

The sample (nanocomposites) was individually tested against bacterial strains, including Gram-positive bacteria: Bacillus subtilus JS-2004 (B. subtilis) JS-2004, Gram-negative bacteria: Escherichia coli (E. coli) ATCC 25,922 The product's identity and purity were verified by the University of Agriculture, Faisalabad, Pakistan, Institute of Microbiology. In Nutrient agar (Oxoid, UK), bacterial strains were cultured overnight at 37 °C. The disc diffusion method was used to assess the antibacterial activity of the sample. Briefly, 100 µL of the microorganism suspension under examination, with 107 colony-forming units (CFU) per milliliter of bacterial cells on nutritional agar medium. Each filter disc (6 mm in diameter) on the agar plates which had already been inoculated with the microorganisms under test was individually injected with the sample solution. Negative control included discs with no sample. Rifimpacin (30 µg/dish) (Oxoid, UK) was utilized as a positive reference for bacteria in order to assess the sensitivity of strains/isolates in the studied microbial species. After 2 h at 4 ºC, plates were incubated for bacteria strains at 37 ºC for 18 h. By measuring the growth inhibition zones (zone reader) for each organism in millimeters and comparing the results to the control, antibacterial activity was ascertained [32–34].

Hemolytic activity of the sample was investigated. From the Department of Clinical Medicine and Surgery at the University of Agriculture in Faisalabad, Pakistan, 3 mL of recently heparinized cow blood were collected. Blood was centrifuged for 5 min at 1000xg. After discarding the plasma, 5 mL of sterile, isotonic Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) that had been chilled to 4 °C was used to wash the cells three times. 7.4 pH. Erythrocytes were kept at 108 cells per mL. Separately, 100 μL of sample was combined with 108 cells/mL of human cells, incubated for 35 min at 37 °C, and then stirred for 10 min. After incubation, the sample was immediately placed on ice for 5 min then centrifuged for 5 min at 1000xg. From each tube 100 μL supernatant was collected and diluted 10 times with chilled (4 °C) PBS. As a positive control, Triton X-100 (0.1 % v/v) was used and for negative control phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was used which pass through the same process. By using μQuant (Bioteck, USA) the absorbance was noted at 576 nm. The% RBCs lysis was calculated of the sample [35,36].

For the first time, a straightforward, environmentally friendly one-step hydrothermal carbonization process has been described for producing quasi spherical carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Moringa oleifera gum powder as a carbon source with superior capabilities. To increase the photocatalytic and chemocatalytic activities, ZnO/CQD nanocomposites are created by methodically combining carbon quantum dots with ZnO nanoparticles using a sonication technique.

UV-visible spectrum analysis of CQDs carried out to study optical properties. Absorption measurements were done in quartz cuvettes and measurements were recorded via deionized water as a reference from 200 to 600 nm (Fig. 2 a). From UV Visible spectra of CQDs, It is evident that it displays two absorption peaks at 278 nm and 223 nm, which are ascribed to the C=O bond's n-π* transition and the C = C bond's π-π* transition, respectively [37,38].

Moringa gum act as reducing and stabilizing agent for the synthesis of nanoparticles and it consist of polysaccharides such as d- mannose, d- xylose, d- galactose, l-rhamnose and l- arabinose as well as hydroxyl and carboxylic functional groups [39]. Fig. 2b shows the UV- visible absorption spectrum of ZnO nanoparticles. The absorption spectrum was measured for the sample between 200 and 800 nm. The spectrum has the absorption peak at 360 nm corresponding to the characteristic band of zinc oxide nanoparticles [40]. The UV-visible absorption spectrum of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites displays a weaker absorption edge at 360 nm of ZnO and two absorption peaks at 278 nm caused by the n-π* electron transition of the C=O bond (Fig. 2c) [29,38]. Compared to pure ZnO nanoparticles, ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites exhibit superior photocatalytic activity under visible light due to their continuous wide absorption in the visible spectrum [11]. Fig. 3
shows the calculated bandgap of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite that is 5.08 eV.

The FT-IR spectroscopy identified the existence of functional groups on the surface of prepared CQDs, ZnO, and ZnO/CQDs. Fig. 4 a shows the FTIR spectrum of CQDs in which a broad absorption band at 3349 cm−1 corresponds to the phenolic OH stretch. The peak at 1581 cm−1 shows the stretching vibration of C=O of aldehyde group. The bands at 1390 cm−1 and 1109 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of C-O-C group [10] and Ar-H is found at 628 cm−1. The FT-IR spectrum of ZnO showed characteristic peaks at 3411, 1634, 1384, 1111, 881, and 655 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 4b. A broad band at 3411 cm−1 assigns to O-H, stretching vibration, a band at 1634 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 are due to bending vibration of adsorbed water molecules on ZnO surface [41] and 1111 shows C=O stretching. Similarly, the absorption bands at 881 cm−1 and 655 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Zn–O.

The FT-IR spectrum of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites shows characteristic peaks at 3545, 1558, 1402, 1099, and 693 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 4c. A broad absorption band at 3545 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H, a band at 1558 and 1402 shows the stretching vibration of COO− which reveals the existence of CQDs on the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites. The peaks at 1099 cm−1 and 693 cm−1 correspond to the C-O and Zn-O-C bonds respectively [11]. Hence, it could be concluded that O-H band is present in all spectrums, but the other bands were shifted to slightly changes in absorbance region which signifies the formation of composites.

In order to confirm the formation of CQDs, EDX analysis was performed. During the EDX different areas were focused and the corresponding peaks are shown in Fig. 7 . EDX spectrum confirmed the presence of C in the synthesized compound as well as O, S, Mg and Na are also detected due to presence of different elements in the Moringa Oleifera gum. The apparent concentration of carbon, oxygen, sodium, magnesium, and Sulphur is 39.02, 44.31, 1.3, 4.1 and 0.7 respectively. Details of the EDX spectra of CQDs values measured in weight% and weight% sigma are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD diffraction study of CQDs and ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: XRD diffraction pattern of CQDs and ZnO/CQDs is shown in the Fig. 8 . The XRD pattern of CQDs shows both broad and sharp peaks. Sharp diffraction peaks indicate carbon quantum dots with better crystallinity and broad peak indicates that the carbon material is organic and amorphous [42]. The XRD pattern of ZnO/CQDs shows seven main peaks of ZnO, which corresponds to crystal planes (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) and the data is in good agreement with hexagonal ZnO (JCPDS No 5–0664), which shows that the synthesized ZnO is of high purity [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue dye
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and pure ZnO nanoparticles is examined using the methylene blue dye as a model pollutant. The UV-visible spectrum of methylene blue dye shows maximum absorption peak at 663 nm [44]. In photocatalytic experiment ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and pure ZnO nanoparticles are used as photocatalyst and it was seen that the peak at 663 nm decreased with respect to time under visible light irradiation. During 150 min, the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites photocatalyst was able to remove about 68 % of the MB concentration (Fig. 9 a). Whereas, in the presence of pure ZnO nanoparticles photocatalyst 42 % of MB concentration was removed (Fig. 9b). The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 10 .

The control experiments comprise the degradation of the MB dye only in the presence of visible light. It can be visualized that visible light alone could not degrade the dye even after 150 min. It only degrades 14 % of MB concentration (Fig. 9c). Hence, it could be concluded that ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites proficiently enhance the photocatalytic activity due to better transfer of electron hole pairs and effective generation of free radicals on the photocatalysts' surface [30]. Such innovative composites could provide fresh insights in the construction of extremely efficient catalysts for pollution control and prospective photoelectric applications [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemo catalytic degradation of methylene blue
ce:section.ce:para: Using sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and methylene blue dye, the catalytic activity of the produced ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and pure ZnO nanoparticles is investigated. Because of its strong electron injection capability of the BH− 4 ions, methylene blue dye was employed as a model pollutant to assess the catalytic activity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and pure ZnO nanoparticles using (NaBH4) as the reducing agent. [45]. The UV-visible spectrum of methylene blue dye shows maximum absorption peak at 663 nm [44]. It was seen that the peak at 663 nm decreased with respect to time and 94 % of the MB concentration was removed in the presence of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and NaBH4 (Fig. 11 a).

Methylene blue in an oxidizing environment undergoes a definite color change, eventually turning colorless with decrease in absorption intensity within 20 min indicating its reduction to leuco-methylene blue [45]. Whereas, in the presence of pure ZnO nanoparticles and NaBH4 the peak at 663 nm decreased very slowly with respect to time and 33 % of the MB concentration was removed. So, ZnO nanoparticles shows very slow degradation than ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites and methylene blue did not fully degrade even after 80 min (Fig. 11b). The mechanism of chemo catalytic activity of ZnO/CQDs is shown in Fig. 12 .

The control experiments comprise the decolorization of the dye only in the presence of sodium borohydride. It is evident that even after 80 min; sodium borohydride was unable to completely breakdown the color. The control experiment reduced only 28 % of the MB concentration (Fig. 11c). Hence, it could be concluded that ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites proficiently catalyzed the reduction of methylene blue dye by imparting electron from BH4 – to methylene blue for the degradation process [46]. From this study, it is evident that ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites could have a pivotal use in the field of environmental remediation for the creation of nanocatalysts [47].

In order to check the long-term viability and reliability of photocatalytic procedures for a range of applications, including organic pollutants degradation, atmospheric pollution management, and water purification, stability tests in photocatalysis and chemo catalysis are essential. The main aim behind stability studies is to check out how many times the photocatalyst may be used before losing its catalytic function [48].

Three reusability tests were carried out to assess the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite's chemo catalytic stability. Fresh MB solution was added and the catalyst was filtered and properly cleaned with deionized water following each cycle. Three reuses of the ZnO/CQDs composite were achieved without any obvious reduction in the methylene blue degrading activity. After three successive cycles, the percentage degradation remained higher than 89 %. It undergoes three rounds of degradation tests before demonstrating its structural stability (Fig. 13 a).

Three reusability tests were also carried out to check the ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite's photocatalytic stability. It's interesting to note that despite these cycles of degradation, its stability was preserved with hardly noticeable changes. Three repetitions of the same experimental protocol were used with fresh MB solution. The composite was successfully reused three times without any appreciable decrease in activity. The degradation rate was greater than 65 % for all three trials. The ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites demonstrated good chemo and photocatalytic stability (Fig. 13b).

The antibacterial activity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite was checked against staphylococcus aureus and E. coli bacteria with positive control of ciprofloxacin. The results have been shown in Table 2 along with the depiction in Fig. 14 respectively. Whereas, the hemolytic activity of ZnO/CQDs nanocomposite has been depicted in Table 3 given below.

It is concluded that green, fluorescent CQDs prepared via Moringa Oleifera gum powder by one-step hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) process. The as prepared CQDs coupled with ZnO nanoparticles to form ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites that are used to improve the photocatalytic and chemocatalytic properties. Methylene Blue is used as a model pollutant to investigate the photocatalytic and chemo catalytic properties of prepared samples. As compared to ZnO nanoparticles, ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites showed improved photocatalytic activity for MB degradation under visible light due to peculiar up-converted photoluminescence properties of CQDs. Furthermore, CQDs can stimulate the creation and separation of photo-generated electron–hole pairs, which increase the number of electrons and holes available for degradation. ZnO/CQDs nanocomposites also showed enhanced chemocatalytic activity towards model pollutant MB degradation than pure ZnO nanoparticles. Such innovative composites could provide fresh insights to the construction of extremely efficient catalysts for pollution control and prospective photoelectric applications.

Shehla Parveen: Writing – original draft. Nida Latif: Visualization. Ghayoor Abbas Chotana: Formal analysis. Aqsa Kanwal: Data curation. Shahid Hussain: Validation. Mohamed A. Habila: Funding acquisition. Amjad Iqbal: . Rajesh Kumar Manavalan: . Nosheen Farooq: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Researchers Supporting Project Number (RSP2024R441), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. One of the authors, Rajesh Kumar Manavalan thanks the contract no 40/is2 and gratefully acknowledges research funding from the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation (Ural Federal University project within the Priority 2030 Program).
